name: Olympiad Inequalities — AM–GM & Cauchy–Schwarz
whatIsIt: Two power-tools for proving one quantity is bigger than another. The AM–GM INEQUALITY says the Arithmetic Mean is always at least the Geometric Mean: for non-negative numbers, (a + b)/2 ≥ √(ab), with equality only when a = b. The CAUCHY–SCHWARZ inequality says (a² + b²)(x² + y²) ≥ (ax + by)². These prove minimums/maximums instantly — e.g. x + 1/x ≥ 2 for positive x.
whyNeeded: Olympiad problems constantly ask to prove a minimum, maximum, or that one expression beats another. AM–GM and Cauchy–Schwarz turn these into one clean line instead of calculus.
realLifeUses: Showing a fixed perimeter gives the biggest area for a square
Finding the least material for a fixed volume
Proving the cheapest split of a fixed total
Bounding errors and averages in science

vocabulary.meaning: AM ≥ GM, with equality only when all the numbers are equal.

story.title: The Fence and the Field
story.text: Rahul has a fixed length of fence and wants the BIGGEST rectangular field. Coach says, 'AM–GM! For a fixed perimeter, area is largest when the sides are equal — a square.' Then Rahul asks, 'What's the smallest value of x + 1/x for positive x?' Coach smiles: 'By AM–GM, x + 1/x ≥ 2√(x·1/x) = 2, equal when x = 1. So the minimum is 2 — no calculus needed.'
story.extractedProblem: For positive x, what is the smallest value of x + 1/x?
story.answerInStory: 2 — by AM–GM, x + 1/x ≥ 2√(x·1/x) = 2, with equality at x = 1.

visual.caption: For a = 2, b = 8: the Arithmetic Mean (5) is at least the Geometric Mean (4). AM–GM says this always holds, with equality only when the numbers are equal.

teachingGallery.title: AM never below GM
teachingGallery.note: Equal only when the numbers match.

examples.caption: The more unequal, the bigger the gap.

standardMethod.summary: To bound a sum from below or a product from above, apply AM ≥ GM (equality when the terms are equal). For sums of products, use Cauchy–Schwarz. The minimum/maximum happens at the equality case.
steps: Spot a sum you want to make small (or a product to make large).
Apply AM ≥ GM: the average is at least the root of the product.
The minimum of a sum with fixed product is 2√(product) (for two terms).
Equality — the actual min/max — happens when all the terms are equal.

workedExamples.problem: Check AM ≥ GM for a = 2, b = 8, and give the geometric mean.
For positive x, find the minimum of x + 9/x.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing GM ≥ AM (the wrong way round).
commonMistakes.fix: It is AM ≥ GM: the arithmetic mean is at least the geometric mean.
AM–GM needs NON-NEGATIVE numbers; it can fail if a term is negative.

easy.q: AM–GM says the arithmetic mean is…
For x > 0, the minimum of x + 1/x is…

medium.q: The geometric mean of 4 and 9 is √(4×9) = ?

questions.q: AM–GM equality happens when the numbers are…

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up why x + 1/x can never be less than 2 for positive x, using AM–GM (average of x and 1/x is at least √(x·1/x) = 1, so the sum is at least 2). Point out equality happens at x = 1.

revisionCard.summary: AM–GM: for non-negative numbers, (a + b)/2 ≥ √(ab), equality when a = b. Sum with fixed product is smallest when terms are equal (x + k/x ≥ 2√k). Cauchy–Schwarz: (a²+b²)(x²+y²) ≥ (ax+by)². Use them to prove min/max without calculus.

rememberIt.hook: AM ≥ GM: (a+b)/2 ≥ √(ab), equal when a = b
rememberIt.unpack: Non-negative only. Min of x + k/x is 2√k, at x = √k. Cauchy: (Σa²)(Σx²) ≥ (Σax)².

studentQuestions.q: When do I use Cauchy–Schwarz instead of AM–GM?
studentQuestions.a: When the expression is a sum of products, like ax + by — Cauchy bounds it using a²+b² and x²+y².

studentQuestions.q: Why does equal-terms give the extreme?
studentQuestions.a: The gap between AM and GM shrinks to zero exactly when the numbers are equal, so that's where the minimum (or maximum) sits.

formulas.name: AM–GM
formulas.whenToUse: Min of a sum / max of a product.

formulas.name: Cauchy–Schwarz
formulas.remember: product of squares ≥ square of dot
